examples.title: Vulnerable Code - strcpy
examples.description: Example of unsafe code susceptible to buffer overflow
examples.explanation: The strcpy function copies user_input to buffer without checking size. If input exceeds 50 characters, it overwrites adjacent stack memory including the return address.

examples.title: Secure Code - strcpy_s
examples.explanation: strcpy_s validates that the source string fits in the destination buffer before copying. If not, it returns an error without causing undefined behavior.

examples.title: Safe String Handling in Python
examples.description: Demonstrating automatic bounds checking in Python
examples.code:
# Python handles bounds checking automatically
def process_user_input(user_input):
    # Python lists/strings resize automatically
    # No buffer overflow possible
    buffer = []
    for char in user_input:
        buffer.append(char)
    
    # Alternative: slicing
    result = user_input[:50]  # Automatically truncates
    
    print(f"Input: {result}")

# Test with arbitrarily long input - still safe
process_user_input("A" * 10000)  # Works fine
examples.explanation: Python automatically handles memory management and includes bounds checking. Buffer overflows are virtually impossible in pure Python code.

examples.title: Stack Canary Implementation
examples.description: How stack canaries are inserted by compiler
examples.explanation: The -fstack-protector flag causes compiler to insert a canary value (typically 0xFF00) between local variables and return address. Before function returns, it verifies the canary hasn't been modified.
